quiz_questions.question_text: A constant force of 10 N pushes an object 5 m along the direction of the force. How much work is done?
quiz_questions.explanation: Work = F * d * cos(0°) = 10 N * 5 m * 1 = 50 J.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 3 kg object moves at 4 m/s. What is its kinetic energy?
A force of 20 N is applied at 60° to the displacement over a distance of 4 m. What is the work done?
A spring with k = 200 N/m is compressed 0.3 m from equilibrium. What is the work done by the spring force when it returns to equilibrium?
quiz_questions.explanation: The work done by a spring returning to equilibrium is W = ½kx² = ½(200)(0.3²) = ½(200)(0.09) = 9 J.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 2 kg object initially at rest has 50 J of net work done on it. What is its final speed?
quiz_questions.explanation: By the Work-Energy Theorem, W_net = ΔKE = ½mv². So 50 = ½(2)v², giving v² = 50, and v ≈ 7.07 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A force F(x) = 6x N acts on a particle from x = 0 to x = 3 m. What is the total work done?
The net work done on a 5 kg object changes its speed from 2 m/s to 6 m/s. What is the net work?
A force does -30 J of work on a moving object. What happens to its kinetic energy?
quiz_questions.explanation: By the Work-Energy Theorem, W_net = ΔKE. Negative work means the kinetic energy decreases by 30 J.

quiz_questions.question_text: A spring with k = 400 N/m is stretched from x = 0.1 m to x = 0.3 m. How much work is done by the applied force?
quiz_questions.explanation: Work done by the applied force on a spring = ½k(x₂² - x₁²) = ½(400)(0.09 - 0.01) = 200(0.08) = 16 J.

quiz_questions.question_text: If the kinetic energy of a particle doubles, by what factor does its speed increase?
quiz_questions.explanation: KE = ½mv². If KE doubles, v² doubles, so v increases by a factor of √2.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of work in physics?

options.text: The product of mass and velocity
The integral of force with respect to displacement along the path: W = ∫F·dr
The rate at which energy is transferred per unit time
The force exerted per unit area on a surface

quiz_questions.explanation: Work is defined as the integral of force dot displacement: W = ∫F·dr. For a constant force, this simplifies to W = Fd cos θ.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the Work-Energy Theorem state?

options.text: Energy is always conserved in all systems
The net work done on an object equals the change in its kinetic energy
Work done equals the change in potential energy only
The total momentum of a system is constant

quiz_questions.explanation: The Work-Energy Theorem states W_net = ΔKE = ½mv_f² - ½mv_i², meaning the net work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is kinetic energy?

options.text: Energy stored in a spring due to compression or stretching
Energy associated with an object's position in a gravitational field
The energy an object possesses due to its motion, equal to ½mv²
The energy transferred by heat between two objects

quiz_questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is the energy of motion, given by KE = ½mv², where m is mass and v is speed.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does Hooke's Law describe?

options.text: The gravitational force between two masses
The restoring force of an ideal spring is proportional to displacement: F = -kx
The relationship between work and kinetic energy change
The conservation of total energy in a closed system

quiz_questions.explanation: Hooke's Law states F = -kx, meaning the restoring force exerted by an ideal spring is directly proportional to the displacement from equilibrium and opposite in direction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is meant by a 'variable force' in the context of work calculations?

options.text: A force that is always perpendicular to the displacement
A force whose magnitude or direction changes with position, requiring integration to compute work
A force that does no work on a system
A force that only acts during collisions

quiz_questions.explanation: A variable force is one whose magnitude or direction changes with position. Work by such a force must be computed using integration: W = ∫F·dr.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the joule (J) as a unit of work or energy?

options.text: The work done when a 1 N force moves an object 1 m in the direction of the force
The energy required to raise 1 kg by 1 m against gravity
The force needed to accelerate 1 kg at 1 m/s²
The power produced when 1 J of work is done per minute

quiz_questions.explanation: One joule is defined as the work done when a force of 1 newton displaces an object by 1 meter in the direction of the force: 1 J = 1 N·m.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does it mean for work to be negative?

options.text: The force is applied in the same direction as the displacement
The force has a component opposite to the direction of displacement, reducing kinetic energy
No energy transfer occurs between the force and the object
The object moves in a circle at constant speed

quiz_questions.explanation: Negative work occurs when the force has a component opposite to the displacement (angle > 90°), which reduces the object's kinetic energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of the spring constant k?

options.text: The maximum force a spring can exert before breaking
The natural length of the spring at equilibrium
A measure of a spring's stiffness, equal to the force per unit displacement
The total energy stored in a fully compressed spring

quiz_questions.explanation: The spring constant k measures stiffness. It equals the force needed per unit displacement (k = F/x), with units of N/m.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a line integral in the context of work?

options.text: An integral that only applies to straight-line motion
The integral of a force along a specified path from one point to another: W = ∫F·dr
The area under a velocity-time graph
An approximation method for computing work with constant forces only

quiz_questions.explanation: A line integral computes the work done by a force along a specified path, accounting for variations in force magnitude and direction: W = ∫F·dr.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'net work' mean?

options.text: The work done by gravity alone on an object
The total work done by all forces acting on an object
The work done by friction only
The work done during the first half of the motion only

quiz_questions.explanation: Net work is the total (algebraic sum) of the work done by all forces acting on an object. By the Work-Energy Theorem, W_net = ΔKE.

quiz_questions.question_text: A warehouse worker pushes a 40 kg crate across a rough floor with a constant horizontal force of 120 N over 8 m. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the crate and the floor is 0.25. Using g = 10 m/s² and assuming the crate starts from rest, what is the speed of the crate after 8 m?
quiz_questions.explanation: Friction force = μmg = 0.25(40)(10) = 100 N. Net work = (120 - 100)(8) = 160 J. By the Work-Energy Theorem, 160 = ½(40)v², so v² = 8, and v = 2√5 ≈ 4.47 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: An engineer designs a spring-loaded launcher for a 0.5 kg ball. The spring has k = 800 N/m and is compressed 0.15 m. If the ball is launched horizontally from a table 0.45 m high, how far from the base of the table does the ball land? (Use g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: Spring PE = ½kx² = ½(800)(0.15²) = 9 J. This becomes KE: 9 = ½(0.5)v², so v² = 36, v = 6 m/s. Fall time: 0.45 = ½(10)t², t² = 0.09, t = 0.3 s. Horizontal distance = 6 × 0.3 = 1.8 m.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 1200 kg car traveling at 25 m/s on a flat highway applies the brakes. The braking force is 6000 N. Using the Work-Energy Theorem, what distance does the car need to stop?
quiz_questions.explanation: W_net = ΔKE. The braking force does negative work: -6000d = 0 - ½(1200)(625) = -375000 J. So d = 375000/6000 = 62.5 m.

quiz_questions.question_text: A bungee cord has a force-extension relationship F(x) = 50x + 100x² (in newtons, with x in meters). A jumper stretches the cord from 0 to 2 m. How much work does the cord do on the jumper?
quiz_questions.explanation: The cord pulls back (restoring force), so work done by the cord on the jumper is negative. W = -∫₀² (50x + 100x²)dx = -[25x² + (100/3)x³]₀² = -[25(4) + (100/3)(8)] = -[100 + 266.7] = -366.7 J.

quiz_questions.question_text: A roller coaster car (mass 500 kg) is at the top of a 20 m hill moving at 5 m/s. Friction does -34,000 J of work as the car descends to the bottom. What is the car's speed at the bottom? (Use g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: Using the Work-Energy Theorem: ½mv_f² = ½mv_i² + mgh + W_friction. ½(500)v_f² = ½(500)(25) + 500(10)(20) + (-34000) = 6250 + 100000 - 34000 = 72250. So v_f² = 72250/250 = 289, and v_f = 17 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A factory conveyor belt lifts 50 kg packages at a constant speed through a height of 3 m while also moving them 4 m horizontally. The belt exerts a constant 500 N force at an angle of 37° above horizontal. How much work does the belt do on each package? (cos 37° = 0.8, sin 37° = 0.6)
quiz_questions.explanation: The total displacement along the belt = √(3² + 4²) = 5 m. Work = F × d × cos(0°) since the force is along the belt = 500 × 5 = 2500 J.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physics student attaches a force sensor to a cart on a track. The sensor records F(x) = 12 - 2x (in newtons) as the cart moves from x = 0 to x = 6 m. What is the total work done by this force?

A crane lifts a 200 kg steel beam at constant velocity to a height of 15 m while wind exerts a constant horizontal force of 300 N. The beam also moves 4 m horizontally during the lift. Using g = 10 m/s², what is the total work done by the crane's cable tension on the beam?
quiz_questions.explanation: At constant velocity, the net work is zero. The cable must do work against gravity (mgh = 200(10)(15) = 30,000 J) and against wind (300 × 4 = 1200 J). Total work by cable = 30,000 + 1,200 = 31,200 J.

quiz_questions.question_text: A delivery truck (mass 2000 kg) traveling at 20 m/s enters a section of road with a rough patch 25 m long (μ_k = 0.4) followed by a smooth section. After passing the rough patch, what is the truck's speed? (Use g = 10 m/s²)

An archer pulls back a bow that behaves like a spring with k = 500 N/m. She draws the string 0.6 m. The 0.05 kg arrow is launched horizontally from a height of 1.8 m above the ground. What is the arrow's speed just as it leaves the bow, and approximately how far downrange does it land? (Use g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: 60 m/s launch speed; lands about 36 m away
30 m/s launch speed; lands about 18 m away
42 m/s launch speed; lands about 25 m away
60 m/s launch speed; lands about 60 m away

quiz_questions.explanation: Spring PE = ½kx² = ½(500)(0.36) = 90 J. Setting equal to KE: 90 = ½(0.05)v², v² = 3600, v = 60 m/s. Fall time: 1.8 = ½(10)t², t = 0.6 s. Range = 60 × 0.6 = 36 m.
